speaker: interviewee
text: That is number A-33. It was tattooed in Blechhammer-- and it was at the beginning of 1943, when Blechhammer was taken over by the SS, and it became a concentration camp, whereas before it was a-- an arbeitslager, a work camp. I was the 33rd in line. This does not indicate in any way that I was the 33rd when they started tattooing. They tattooed from one to 200,000, six digits, and down they started with series A, which went to 20,000. And then after 20,000, they started with series B. At first, when they tattooed, they put the people out by their-- by their letter, alphabetically. But when they came to series A, they just rounded us up, stood in the line. A cousin of mine that lives in Jerusalem, he has A-14. Now, there's very few left with these numbers.

speaker: interviewer
text: But everywhere I go, even among our own people. Ironically, I met a guy last week who was in the same camp. And he says, how come you have such a small number? I know you were in the same camp as I was. Well, he was in camp there before when I came from the other camp. Anyway, this is-- I was at 33rd in line so I have A-33. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you return to Poland at any time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- all these years since the liberation, I could not see why one would want to go back to Poland. I ridiculed them, I-- I argued with them fiercely. I says, why are you going back? Are you going to find somebody? Are you going to find a-- a neighbor, are you going to find a cousin, an uncle, an acquaintance, a friend that you went to school with? What's the sense going back?

speaker: interviewer
text: Besides, after all what they did to us, how can you-- how can you possibly think of such a thing? My brother went to-- my brother went back to Poland in 1974 and I-- I couldn't believe that he really wanted to go back. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] finally it started getting to me, too. I started to realize I'm getting old and everybody wants-- has a sub human power that draws them to place of his origin, his or her origin. And I got to realize that it's getting late. In 1991, I decided I want to go back to Poland. Could you tell us about your-- your visit?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- it was a-- an event that I'll never forget. And not only this, I-- after my first visit, I have a very strong, strong urge to go back again because the things I-- I've left my heart there. I-- I-- I feel-- I felt much closer to my people while I was there, even though they were not around physically. But I felt much closer spiritually and emotionally. And that's what's urging me to go back. And then also it's getting late in life. Who knows, another year or two I may not be able to walk. Here's what I wrote after coming back from Poland. By the way, I was going to go by myself and my brother did not want me to go out on myself and he joined me. The two of us went to Poland in 1991, at the end of '91. And these are the impressions I've gotten. 'I have just returned from a trip to Poland. My brother, Henry, and I spent seven days in Poland. Then we flew to Israel on an El Al from Warsaw to Tel Aviv. We wanted to see what beautiful Bendin looks like today, after not being there since the end of 1942. We flew into Warsaw on a Tuesday-- on a Thursday-- and the next day we went by train to Katowice The train ride-- Katowice is not far from my hometown, which is a much bigger town. The train ride was very comfortable. We were only three people in the compartment that seats six people. The landscape was very bleak and gray. The air was polluted to such a degree that my throat was scratchy, which lasted during most of our stay in Poland. Our companion on the train was a nice young Pole, an engineer by profession. He was very anxious to start up a conversation right from the very beginning, as we settled ourselves on that train for a three and a half to four-hour journey to Katowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: This young man seemed to be very self-conscious over the political situation in his country and very self-composed. Without telling him that we are Jews, he could not contain his emotions in wonderment and asked us, why does the whole world condemn the Poles for having helped the Germans exterminate the Jews? Didn't the French, didn't the Romanians, didn't the Hungarians, the Lithuanians, and the Ukrainians help the Germans in their killings? Didn't the Germans also kill the Poles? Of course, the tone of the conversation was a very polite one. And I just couldn't rebuke his argument by asking, why were most of the death factories in Poland-- such as Auschwitz, Treblinka, Majdanek, [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], et cetera, et cetera? At another time and another place, the answer to the same question was because Poland had the largest concentration of Jews and also the Germans built the death factories also for the Poles.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too convincing for me. Our companion on the train bid us goodbye, wished us well, and gave us some good advice how to move in and around Katowice. Very friendly.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell Katowice, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-A-T-O-W-I-C-E. 'Katowice, which is in [? older ?] Silesia, which is only a couple of kilometers from our hometown. This was the place where we actually stayed in a hotel. We checked into our hotel not far from the railroad station in Katowice and proceeded by cab to Bendin. On the way, we passed a football field. The sign outside reads, [POLISH] football field. That is the former [POLISH] soccer field where the first punkt, where the first selection, started, where all the torture and all the terror started. The mood is depressing. Your heart is pounding and racing as we disembark at the railroad station in Bendin, Bendzin, [INAUDIBLE] the city of Bendin. We start walking on the [INAUDIBLE]. Everything looks like miniature replicas. Incredible. Everything looks so small, so dirty. All the facades of the buildings are covered with soot and crumbling plastic exposes the original bricks. The windows are broken and the pavements are unrepaired. Not too many people in the streets. It looks like the new owners of these buildings have not spent a nickel's worth of paint or repair since they took them over from the former Jewish owners. Incidentally, the expression [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which means our streets but your properties came up many times in our travels in Poland.' I'll skip all these here minute descriptions of the streets which wound in and out, which may not be of very importance. But I'll continue here. 'We went back to [INAUDIBLE] Street, and we went where the [? Nunbergs ?] had the sausage factory, and we went back to the other street. And all you see, dilapidated, torn down, black walls with noticeable marks where you can see where the second floors and then the third floors were. We are walking through the [INAUDIBLE] and-- Street-- and we walk through the gymnasium that was named after [INAUDIBLE], one of the prominent Jewish rich people, philanthropists, in our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we are standing on a railroad bridge not far from the prison and not far-- and we are nearing the school where we went as kids. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We are nearing the school and we are wondering, is the school still there or not? But we-- we see some kids coming out, and as we see some kids, we follow their trail, and lo and behold, there is the building.

speaker: interviewee
text: The building, we walk inside the sports place where we used to ice skate in the wintertime. Where the entrance to the school was at the rear of the building, now the entrance is at the front of the building. We walked around it and there are still some classes in session. We walk into the first floor when a feeling overcame me, just as it was in the middle '30s when we attended the school, except the tile floors which used to shine like mirrors were half broken, dirty and the walls were lacking a good paint job. We remembered the principal's office and proceeded to go up to the second floor and looked around. Some teachers watched us, but nobody stopped us. We went up to the third floor. I remembered how our orchestra, in which I played, used to sit on the landing between the second and third floors and on every holiday like Hanukkah or Purim, we used to play holiday music. My heart was jumping. I was highly emotional. On the way down, one young woman teacher finally approached us and asking who we were looking for. We told her we once attended the school. She was very kind and asked us a few questions. I could not contain my feelings any longer. I broke down and was overcome by emotions. Seeing this, she grabbed us by our arms and insisted that we come up into the principal's office. She got all excited and tried to get in touch with the head of the school. She summoned a few other teachers, put up tea, served us drinks, and we just sat there for a good while reminiscing. They wanted us to come back the next morning and speak in front of the school assembly, but unfortunately we could not make it. Upon bidding us goodbye, they kissed us and pleaded with me to send a copy of a picture that I told her I have at home of the orchestra, which Mr. [INAUDIBLE], the orchestra leader, was in charge. The reception we received there was just beautiful. It was a day I will never forget. We took the train back to Katowice, where we stayed overnight. Katowice was once a beautiful city. It still is, except all the facades of the buildings are covered with soot and dirt. There are hundreds of people milling about the train station and the stench of urine is unbearable. The people look like characters from Dante's According to our taxi driver, those are foreigners who became street people, mostly Hungarians and Russians. During the German occupation the headquarters and executive offices from Auschwitz and Birkenau must have been in Katowice. Wherever I look, I see images of guys dressed in Nazi uniforms. The doorman in the lobby of our hotel tells us to walk around the block from the hotel. There's a monument for all the Jews from Katowice who perished during the war. When we got there, we saw an impressive monument and a bouquet of fresh flowers at the foot of the monument, which was really moving. The next day, we returned to Bendin, the second time. We went to the cemetery. The cemetery has partially been restored as of late by a former Bendiner who resides in Frankfurt, Germany, who has spent a small fortune doing it. As a matter of fact, as we were there, we witnessed a crew of several people using landscaping equipment and clearing the main path, working feverishly. A fence has been put up, and I couldn't believe how much it looked like as I remembered it. My brother and I split up in different directions, aisles, and we started looking. Every couple of minutes or so, I called him or he called me to show me another of our discoveries. Looking at the [INAUDIBLE], the tombstones, and reading the names on them was an experience I will never forget for the rest of my life. Bendin came alive again. All those familiar names, all those memories. Shortly before leaving the cemetery, I stumbled upon the stone of my grandmother. I never expected to find it. It was then when it really hit home. I cried uncontrollably. I just couldn't get over it, that this beautiful stone withstood six decades of the most tragic events that befell our people. I called my brother and we both stood there in disbelief. I felt like my mother, my aunts, and all the descendants of this great bubbe of ours were looking over my shoulder and watching us. I almost could hear my mother whispering in my ear, [? Joseph, ?] I'm glad you came. Then we climbed out of there. The underbrush and the overgrown branches were so thick that we actually climbed in and out of graves, or what once used to be graves. On the way out, the caretaker of the cemetery asked us if we found what we were looking for, and he told us that they-- that means [INAUDIBLE]-- the man that has restored it-- is trying to catalogue all the names so it will be easier to find who everyone is looking for. We left the cemetery and took another walk to town. At a site where once the great synagogue was situated right in front of the castle, we closed our eyes, again trying to figure out where and what it did-- where it-- what it did look like in our memories. What we saw is the castle neglected like a [INAUDIBLE] on a hillside. I'm so positively sure that this is the site of our synagogue because of the definite landmarks-- the castle and the little river there nearby-- but it is impossible to recognize it. The terrain is hilly, I'm stepping on stones, I'm digging with my shoes, and I discover that these stones are like steps. Those were the steps leading to our beautiful synagogue. Right after the burning down of the synagogue and the whole neighborhood at the very beginning of the war, I was one of the forced labor columns to clear up the ruins of this historical synagogue. I'm standing there, and I simply cannot walk away. It pulls me back. From there, we walk to the [INAUDIBLE], to this place and the other place. There's a young man standing in front of one of the houses that looks like it's falling down, watching us very suspiciously. On the wall behind him is a chalk marked swastika. We ask him if there is an old cemetery that used to be right around there nearby. As he answers affirmatively, there was an old woman coming out of the house saying loudly, oh, Jews are coming. Jews are coming. She tells her that she has been living there all her life and that nobody cared to restore the cemetery so it just disappeared. We left Bendin brokenhearted. I did not say goodbye because I know I will be coming back. I have made a full cycle come around. I felt that I had fulfilled a moral imperative. I had to do it. It is written somewhere that the place of birth pulls the person no matter how far away one is. In a way, it was a feeling of relief. I just cannot explain it. Subconsciously, it was a feeling of defiance. Bendin may be judenrein, free of Jews, but there are still some Bendiner Jews around. My heart and all my senses and every particle of my body was weeping. My soul was tormented as we have transformed our minds, even though temporarily, into the souls of our dear departed fathers and mothers. We look at ourselves as shadows of all our brethren who perished. We left Bendin and went to Auschwitz-Birkenau. We went by train. As we got off the train at [? Auschwentiam ?] City, which is Auschwitz in Polish, a cab driver approached us, asking if we are going to the museum. What an irony. The place where so many Jews were tortured and burned is called a museum. Most of the visitors come in groups by chartered buses. We were there on our own, so we attached our-- attached ourselves to a group of about 15, 20 young Germans, mostly males. The guide was a very articulate gentleman who spoke in very clear German that was beneficial for us to understand him. We walked from block to block listening to the horrors explained by the guide. Nothing was said about Jews. As I watch the young tourists, I'm studying their faces and expressions.

speaker: interviewer
text: They are somber, sincere, and they follow the explanations with serious thoughts. Nobody is saying anything, just following the guide. We walk from one block to another. The streets or the roads are of cobblestone and not easy to walk. The camp seems to be endless and the walking is getting to be tiring and burdensome. What was it like for my mother or for my father?

speaker: interviewee
text: We come across other groups of different nationalities speaking different languages, but no Jewish groups, and hardly any Jews at all, with the exception of one Israeli family. We filled into-- we filed into cellars single file where some of the inmates were tortured. We're shown the crematorium, very assuming, which looks like ovens in a bakery, with rails where the stretchers went gliding straight to the ovens.

speaker: interviewer
text: We enter the blocks where shoes, toothbrushes, shaving brushes, suitcases, crutches, artificial limbs are piled up by the thousands. [INAUDIBLE] to tefillin, I'm looking at these shoes and I'm thinking, where they my father's shoes, perhaps maybe my mother's shoes, my sister's? By now, I'm numb. All my tear ducts have dried out until the next stop.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the tour is over, the guide explains and shows us where there are different blocks dedicated to different nationalities. There's one dedicated to the Jews. My brother and I enter the Jewish block. There's nobody in there except two woman guards. One of them puts on lights for us and a recorded voice of a very good cantor is singing [INAUDIBLE], a prayer for the dead. The voice pierces through your heart. We walk around and look at the exhibits on the walls. In the middle of a big room, there is a hole, a big hole, cut out in the floor in the shape of a grave. You look and see the abyss and the end of a beautiful great Polish Jewry buried right here. The crushing impact on you is like a thunderbolt, reviving you from numbness to the pinnacle of human suffering and back to numbness.

speaker: interviewer
text: When we left the Jewish block, the woman guards shut the lights and closed the doors behind us. I left very angry when I was outside of the building. The reason for my anger was that nobody else was there, just the two of us. Where are the compatriots of ours, where are the Jewish tourists, where are all the people that preach constantly and remind the world not to forget the darkest period in human history? Is it our task only to remind everybody else not to forget?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Auschwitz, we went to Birkenau, three kilometers away. What Birkenau lacked in notoriety compared to Auschwitz, it made up in gruesomeness. This is where the real death factory operated in full force. As you go from barrack to barrack, you see messages scribbled in with human nails on the walls with names on it. You see the cubbyholes where the inmates spent their last hours. It is so overwhelming. It simply electrifies you and the goosebumps actually sting you. Suddenly, you cannot talk and you realize that you are reaching the highest point of human suffering. Nothing else matters anymore. It is horrifying. And then finally, you come to the end of the railroad tracks, where the bombed out crematoriums-- number one, number two, and number three-- are laying in the ruins. They-- there were could not control ourselves. The Germans, by the way, destroyed them in the days before the end of the war. I was looking into the cracks of the ruins and I felt my father's eyes looking right at me, staring straight in my face. It was hard to leave this place. I felt that part of me was buried there. From there we went to Krakow. After we had checked into our hotel there, I remembered reading in Philadelphia in the local paper that there is a Jewish kosher restaurant in Krakow. I inquired at the desk of the hotel as to the location of the restaurant. It took a lot of walking and searching for it. When we finally found the hotel where the kosher restaurant was supposed to have been located, all we could see was a second class hotel lobby, in front of an entrance to a restaurant, the sign read, [SPEAKING GERMAN], 'fish Jewish style.' What a disappointment. So much for the Jewish kosher restaurant. The next day, we took a taxi cab to the main synagogue in Krakow. When we got there, we found ourselves facing a colossal old building of heavy concrete encircled with a heavy iron fence and gates half sunk into the ground like an old matriarch bent down. The gates would not open even if there weren't locked. This shul was not used for decades. We tried to gain entry from different sides, but there was no sign of any movement. We are lost, yet I knew that there still was an active synagogue in Krakow. The neighborhood looks and feels like the ghettos of old, with its architectural layout. We walked and we walked and suddenly, we see a courtyard and within on a bench were sitting a few old people wearing [INAUDIBLE], prayer shawls. We were elated. Finally, we found some mark of Jewish life. That day happened to be Shemini Atzeret, the last day of Sukkot. Also, the day when [INAUDIBLE] memorial services are being recited. That's why the services took place. We entered the synagogue. This happened to be the famous Remuh Synagogue, named after the great sage Remuh by Rabbi Moshe Isserles.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Remuh, R-E-M-U, or Rabbi Moshe Isserles, I-S-E-R-L-E-S, 'a famous sage who lived in the 16th century. The building dates back to 1574 and that one was called the new shul. The one from 1574 was called the new shul. The one we couldn't get inside when we first got there was the old shul. As we looked around, we saw a group of 15 to 20 old people praying. They had just about finished reading the Torah. I was asked if I could assist them in lifting the Torah, and I gladly volunteered. I was also given the honor to conduct the rest of the services. They seemed to have liked my performance very much. As I stood in front of the altar and looked at the walls; the pews, the benches, the architecture, the eternal light, the [NON-ENGLISH], and the matching wrinkled faces of my co-worshippers, I could not help not feeling electrified emotionally when a feeling overwhelmed me. All I could think of was, if only these walls could talk, if they could reveal the events that they were witness of-- the joys and the sorrows, the happiness, the tragedies, the pleasures and misfortunes, the tears that were spilled here, the ecstasy of old prayers, the rivalries among its worshippers, the happy holidays, the brisses, the bar mitzvahs, the weddings, [INAUDIBLE], nights, the [INAUDIBLE] nights, the funerals, the eulogies, and the great rabbis-- the Hazzanim, the cantors, and above all the [INAUDIBLE], the children, the [NON-ENGLISH], the [NON-ENGLISH], the [? Yankele, ?] the [NON-ENGLISH], the [NON-ENGLISH], and the [NON-ENGLISH]. All these scenes suddenly appeared and came to haunt me. I felt like I was surrounded by them, all in one big circle spinning through generations. And then as the circle was breaking up, I was left with a few broken pieces of the remnants of that great Polish Jewry. I was told by the leader of the community that the services should be over by a certain time because the police guarding the synagogue could not afford more manpower to watch it. Because of it being Shemini Atzeret, the holiday, we could not enter the cemetery, which is right in the back of the synagogue, where the Remuh supposedly is buried. As a courtesy to us and appreciation for conducting the services, they took us to the rear of the building and through an open window from the women's section, they showed us the grave-- the tombstone of Remuh and his whole family buried right alongside. This place now is like a mecca for every visitor-- Jewish visitor-- and tourist that visits Jewish historical sites in Krakow. From there, they took us to the old synagogue, the one on which we couldn't enter before. Through a rear entrance, we were led inside through a magnificent huge building that while deteriorating is still so beautiful. I was standing in awe looking up at the ceiling, at the balconies, the architecture, the art, the beautiful artwork, and the hanging lights. All this now laying in ruins, with peeling paint on the walls, rotten windows, broken pews, all these broken chips of a crown that adorned Krakow's Jewry.

speaker: interviewer
text: I could feel the [INAUDIBLE], the holy spirit, still resting and hovering in the air. This edifice was so beautiful that even the Nazis tried to preserve it for the future. One of the officials who took us there-- who was, by the way, active in restoring old Jewish historical sites in Poland-- told me that Mr. Lauder, the famous philanthropist, walked away from there without any positive action because the renovation costs would involve hundreds of thousands of dollars at least. The question is, for whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Krakow had 60,000 Jews before the war and now for [INAUDIBLE] services on that holiday, there were just a handful of old people, the remnants of all this Jewry. There were many more eventful experiences in Krakow, but I will not mention them now. From there, we went to Warsaw. I was never in Warsaw before in my life. By standing inside the cemetery on [INAUDIBLE] Street in [INAUDIBLE] in front of the monuments of [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], all these pillars and greats of pre-war Polish Jewry, again, I could not control myself. I felt my knees buckling again as I was moaning and sobbing. Suddenly, I felt that I was standing in a mass grave of all Jews in Europe, of Europe, at the graves of all the rabbis, scholars, thinkers, philosophers, poets, artists, merchants, physicians, scientists, the most vibrant, creative people, the crown of world Jewry going back centuries buried right here, and with them part of me also buried. It was so hard to part with this place and just walk away. I did walk away so brokenhearted, with such a deep world-- void in my heart I wanted to scream. From there we went to Israel.' And I will just finish off with this here. I will finish right here with this because this is irrelevant. This is not relevant to what we are talking about. There's a few impressions of-- about leaving Poland, but this is it.
